content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMfvIISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 958 12-0807
(916) 322-5660
• Fax (916) 322-0886
June 1, 1998
Howard Stern
City Attorney
City of El Cerrito
10890 San Pablo Avenue
El Cerrito, California 94530
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-98-136
Dear Mr. Stern:
You are writing on behalf of El Cerrito City Councilmember Gina Brusatori. She has
authorized you to write on her behalf in your capacity as city attorney. This letter is in response
to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTION
May Councilmember Brusatori participate in governmental decisions involving the
Lucky Store proposals?
CONCLUSION
Yes. Councilmember Brusatori may participate in governmental decisions involving the
Lucky Store proposals.
FACTS
El Cerrito Councilmember Brusatori is employed by ABN AMRO Bank, N.V., (“ABN”)
in its San Francisco office. The bank is a Dutch institution. Ordinary shares of ABN have been
listed on the New York Stock Exchange in the form of American Depository Receipts. Each
receipt represents one ordinary share.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-98-136
Page 2
Councilmember Brusatori’s job duties include the securing and servicing of commercial
accounts for the bank. Councilmember Brusatori is paid a salary. She is also entitled to bonuses
based on her individual loan performance as well as the loan performance of her unit. If the loan
unit meets or exceeds program goals, Councilmember Brusatori may be eligible for a bonus
based on that achievement.
American Stores Company (“ASC”) is the parent corporation of several retail operations,
including Lucky Stores. ASC would like to construct two new Lucky Stores in the City of El
Cerrito. Both stores will require discretionary land use approvals from the city. It is foreseeable
that Councilmember Brusatori will be called upon to review the environmental documents
prepared for each of these projects and to determine whether a use permit should be issued for
either or both of these projects. Both of these projects are in redevelopment areas.
The El Cerrito City Council also serves as the El Cerrito Redevelopment Agency
(“Agency”). As a voting member ofthe Agency, Councilmember Brusatori may be called upon
to review certain agreements between the Agency and ASC (or one of its subsidiaries), such as
Disposition and Development Agreements and similar agreements. The Agency may also be
asked to determine whether condemnation proceedings should be instituted in regards to either,
or both, of the Lucky Store projects.
In order to provide a backstop for ASC’s commercial paper program, and for general
corporate purposes, ASC has a one and one-halfbillion dollar ($1,500,000,000) line of credit
with a collection ofparticipating banks. ABN is one of the banks which participates in this line
of credit program with ASC. ABN has agreed to make up to ten million dollars ($10,000,000)
available as part ofthis $1.5 billion line of credit program. Inaddition, ABN has not done any
business with ASC on an individual basis during the last 12 months and ASC has no outstanding
loans with ABN.
To secure the credit and choose the lender, ASC would provide notice to the participating
banks ofthe amount of money ASC was seeking under the line of credit. Interested participating
banks would then bid to be selected by ASC. The decision ofwhich bank or banks would
provide the desired credit would rest with ASC.
ASC has no loans outstanding under this line of credit program. According to ASC’s
general counsel, ASC does not anticipate using this line of credit for any purpose in the
foreseeable future. The general counsel also stated that ASC would not utilize the line of credit
in connection with the construction of the new stores. ASC would instead utilize permanent
construction financing for the stores.


File No. A-98-136
Page 3
Though you believe that it is not reasonably foreseeable that ASC would utilize any
credit from ABN, ASC andlor ABN are willing to submit a letter to the El Cerrito City Council
that states that no credit will be given to ASC from ABN for this project. This assurance would
be given to eliminate the possibility of a conflict of interest.
ANALYSIS
Introduction
The Act was adopted by the people of the State of California by initiative in 1974. The
purpose ofthe conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to insure that public officials perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests ofpersons who support them. (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of this goal,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows
or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
Financial Interests
Councilmember Brusatori is a public official. (Section 82048.) Section 87103 specifies
that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member ofhis or her immediate family or on, among other things,
any source of income of $250 or more during the previous 12 months. ANC is a source of
income to Councilmember Brusatori.
Also, Regulation l8702.l(a)(4) specified that Councilmember Brusatori has a financial
interest in the governmental decision if it will result in her or her immediate family’s personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities increasing or decreasing by at least $250 in any 12 month
period. (Section 87103; Regulation 18702.1(a)(4).)
Foreseeabiity
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a
governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty
is not required. However, if an effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in
providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
You have stated that ASC will not seek financing for the Lucky Store proposals from
ABN. Therefore, it is not reasonably foreseeable that there will be a financial effect on ABN.


File No. A-98-136
Page 4
Also, since ASC will not seek financing from ABN, Councilmember Brusatori’s income will not
be affected. Therefore, Councilmember Brusatori may participate in a governmental decision
involving ASC and the Lucky Store proposals.
Materiality
The following discussion is provided only for your information since it has already been
determined that Councihnember Brusatori may participate in a governmental decision involving
ASC and the Lucky Store proposals.
Assuming foreseeability, disqualification is still only required where the foreseeable
effect on the public official’s economic interest is material. The Commission has adopted a
series ofregulations that provide guidance concerning whether the foreseeable financial effects
of a decision are material. (Regulation 18702.) The standard of materiality differs depending on
the type of economic interest involved and whether the economic interest is directly or indirectly
involved in a decision.
2 You seek advice regarding only those governmental decisions where the
councilmember’s economic interest, the banic, is indirectly involved.
When a source of income is a business entity indirectly involved, Regulation 18702
provides the applicable standard ofmateriality. The exact standard depends on the size of the
business entity. Pursuant to Regulation 1 8702.2(a)(1)-(3), the effect of a governmental decision
involving the bank is material if there is either (i) an increase or decrease in the gross revenues of
the bank of at least $250,000, (ii) an increase or decrease of the expenses ofthe bank of at least
$100,000, or (iii) an increase or decrease of the assets or liabilities ofthe bank of at least
$250,000.
2
“A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an
official’s agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent:
(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or:
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official’s agency.
(3) A person or business entity is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license,
permit or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person or business
entity.” (Regulation 18702.1(b).)


File No. A-98-136
Page 5
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
14A7
By:
Marte Castafios
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:MC:jlw

Lucky Store proposals?

Lucky Store proposals.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Councilmember Brusatori participate in governmental decisions involving the
Lucky Store proposals?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Councilmember Brusatori may participate in governmental decisions involving the
Lucky Store proposals.
